coredata.dc:description: The poor tribological performances of titanium alloy as bone implants has important influence on its implantation failure. In this investigation, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) modified MXene (MXene/NCDs) composite coatings with “dual-action” effects were meticulously fabricated on the Ti6Al4V surface through grafting silane coupling agent and dopamine adhesion layers via chemical self-assembly techniques. The friction coefficient and wear rate of MXene/NCDs coated titanium alloy in SBF lubricant significantly decreased by 31.7 %and 91.6 %, might due to the double effect of MXene sheet slipping and NCDs rolling. Furthermore, the enhanced anticorrosion properties of MXene/NCDs coating were attributed to the ionic reaction between the surface of coating and SBF, effective shielding mechanism against the infiltration of active ions within the solution, indicated that the coating is expected to be applied in artificial joint system.

head.ce:title: Improved corrosion resistance and tribological properties of MXene/NCDs coatings on the Ti6Al4V alloys

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poor tribological performances of titanium alloy as bone implants has important influence on its implantation failure. In this investigation, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) modified MXene (MXene/NCDs) composite coatings with “dual-action” effects were meticulously fabricated on the Ti6Al4V surface through grafting silane coupling agent and dopamine adhesion layers via chemical self-assembly techniques. The friction coefficient and wear rate of MXene/NCDs coated titanium alloy in SBF lubricant significantly decreased by 31.7 %and 91.6 %, might due to the double effect of MXene sheet slipping and NCDs rolling. Furthermore, the enhanced anticorrosion properties of MXene/NCDs coating were attributed to the ionic reaction between the surface of coating and SBF, effective shielding mechanism against the infiltration of active ions within the solution, indicated that the coating is expected to be applied in artificial joint system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti6Al4V alloys has garnered substantial attention within the realm of the context of implant and prosthesis fabrication [1–3], due to their light weight and excellent biocompatibility as well as low Young's modulus [4–6], closely resembling that of human bone [7]. Especially, in the artificial joint lubrication system, Ti6Al4V has been as the most popular material. However, during the operation of artificial joint lubrication system, Ti6Al4V exhibits high friction coefficient and high wear rate against friction pairs such as ceramic balls (Si3N4 [8,9], ZrO2 [10], calcium phosphate [11]), ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene [12] and Polyether ether ketone [13], etc. Especially, in the artificial joint lubrication system, Ti6Al4V produced wear debris after friction potentially caused adverse reactions with bone tissues, ultimately leading to aseptic loosening and even implant failure [14,15], limit their applications in the field of bone implants [16–20]. Consequently, the wear resistance of this titanium alloy was enhanced by surface modification techniques [21–24].

Surface coating technology have been regarded as an efficacious and straightforward treatment approach. Various coatings have been developed by researchers to augment the tribological characteristics of the Ti6Al4V substrate. Through laser-based directed energy deposition technology, Xie et al. [25] grafted a 10 nm smooth Ta-Zr alloy coating on Ti6Al4V substrate, and found that the corrosion resistance and wear resistance of Ti6Al4V were advanced. Zhu et al. [26] cladded Co-Cu/Ti3SiC2 coatings on the surface of Ti6Al4V by laser technology, and demonstrated that the wear resistance of coating was improved. Ju et al. [27] prepared a series of novel Ti6Al4V-xCu coatings by laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) for improving the biotribology, bioactivity, antibacterial property, and corrosion resistance. Nanomaterials coatings have been concerned and applied in biomedical field, such as multi-wall carbon nanotube (CMWNT) [28], graphene oxide (GO) [29–31], BN. Among those nanomaterial coatings, MXene as a burgeoning two-dimensional transition metal carbonization material, has attracted the public attention in improving the tribological properties from the biomedical field [32,33]. ARMIN et al. revealed the excellent mechanical properties of MXene material with the higher tensile strength of about 560 MPa and high fracture toughness [34] Minufar et al. indicated the outstanding mechanical properties and biocompatibility of MAX and MXene composites, demonstrating their great promise in the biomedical field [35]. Owing to its mechanical properties are similar to the single-layer graphene-like structure, the abundant surface terminations (-F, -O, -OH) [36], in addition to inherent self-lubrication and relatively low shear strength of MXene [37,38]. Hence, MXene has been widely applied as a reinforcing material to form effective coatings with excellent mechanical properties and solid lubrication properties [39–42]. Zhang et al. [43] found that layer spacing and stacking type of MXs would affect its lubrication performance, proved that larger layer distance and ample oxygen functionalization from MXene and mirror stacking would result from excellent tribological performance. Andreas et al. analyzed that the substrate adhesion of MXene in-situ friction film contributed to passivate contact, significantly reducing interfacial adhesion, thereby achieving low friction and low wear performance [44]. Marquis et al. [45] exploited DFT calculations to investigate that the interlayer interaction and treatment control ends of Ti-based MXenes to avoid the -OH and -F ends can significantly improve its tribological properties as a solid lubricant. In addition, Serles et al. [46] found 2D nanoscale Ti3C2Tx MXene lubricity and the reduction of surface end -OH can improve tribological properties. They also proved its sustainable lubricity, thus making its application in the field of friction longer. To date, MXene has been increasingly used in the field of friction due to its layered structure and self-lubricating properties [32,37]. However, monolithic MXene grafted on the titanium alloy surface has the disadvantage of mechanical brittleness due to the lack of interlayer interaction [47]. Meanwhile, the MXene sheet are highly susceptible to oxidation due to the exposure of metal atoms on its surface [48]. Hence, it can be considered to protect the MXene from further oxidation with biocompatible materials containing more functional groups [49].

The carbon quantum dots (CD) have outstanding lubricity [50–52], corrosion resistance and mechanical properties [53,54], and is widely used in the lubrication field. Qiang et al. [55] evaluated the tribological properties of graphene doped with carbon quantum dots. Quantum dots (CQDs) showed the best tribological properties after being added as a water-based lubricant, reducing the friction coefficient by 42.5 % and the wear rate by 58.5 %. Yin et al. [56] synthesized the 2D Ti3C2/Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) hybrid multicoating via deposition method, showing the ultralow-wear and low COFs for durability. Element doping (such as phosphorus, sulfur, nitrogen) and surface functionalization can be better achieved the more properties of CD. Xiao et al. [57] prepared a sulfur doped carbon quantum dot as a water-based lubrication additive and proved that the modified carbon quantum dots can effectively improve friction and reduce wear. Ren et al. [58] synthesized NCDs as a hard template and added it to acrylic resin, and found that its dispersion performance was good and its corrosion resistance was significantly improved. Wang et al. [59] prepared NCDs by two-step hydrothermal method, revealing its excellent corrosion inhibition ability and strong adsorption ability with carbon steel. Considering the cytotoxicity of sulfur, low cytotoxic nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) was selected to enhance the tribological properties of the coating and its binding force with the substrate [60,61].

In this study, MXene coatings and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) modified MXene coatings (MXene/NCDs) were successfully grafted on the surface of titanium alloys by chemical self-assembly method. In addition, the biotribological and electrochemical corrosion properties of the two samples in SBF solution were tested. MXene/NCDs coatings significantly reduced the wear rate, friction coefficient and improved corrosion inhibition efficiency than MXene coatings. The mechanism of MXene/NCDs coating was analyzed by wear surface analysis and Hertz calculation formula.

The elemental composition of Ti6Al4V alloy was displayed in Table 1 . The foremost titanium alloy (Ti6Al4V) plates were processed into cuboid with the length (20 mm) × width (20 mm) × height (2 mm), and polished to the roughness (Ra) of 0.1 ± 0.02 μm. The Ti6Al4V plates were initially subjected to ultrasonic cleaning with acetone at 150W for 1 h, followed by three rounds of rinsing with deionized water to remove any traces of oil contamination, resulting in pristine samples. Si3N4 balls were purchased from Shang hai JZN Bearing Co., Ltd. The used chemical substances were of analytical grade, including 3-Hydroxytyramine Hydrochloride (DA, 98 %), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %), Tris (hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), Citric acid, Ethylenediamine, NH4OH solution (25 %), Ti3AlC2 (0.5–25 μm, ≥90 wt%) and HF, were purchased from Shanghai Discovery Technology Co., LTD.

Citrate acid (2 g) was mixed into 50 mL of ethylenediamine (1.5 mL) deionized water solution. After stirring for 30 min, the transparent mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (TLSA) at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting product was cooled and dialyzed (MWCO: 3.5 kDa) for 24 h, and freeze-dried for 48 h. The corrected NCDs concentration was about 7.72 mg/mL.

Two dimensional MXene were synthesized by HF etching. Ti3AlC2 (2 g) was slowly added into 100 mL HF (40 wt%) solution and etched for 48 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged with 8000 rpm and washed with deionized water, and centrifuged repeatedly until pH = 7. And then the precipitation dried overnight at 65 °C in a vacuum oven to obtain a thin layer of MXene [57]. The thin layer of MXene powder was stripped by mixing N- methylpyrrolidone (NMP) with a concentration of 10 mg/mL. The MXene/NMP thin film mixture underwent sonication at 400 W for 48 h, followed by centrifugation at a high speed of 12000 rpm for 30 min. Several high-speed cycles washing with deionized water and ethanol completely removed the residual NMP. Two dimensional MXene were prepared and freeze-dried at −60 °C for 48 h. Finally, the MXene were thoroughly stirred in Tris solution (pH = 8.5) of 2 g·L−1 DA at room temperature for 12 h, and add 50 mL NCDs to react again for 6 h. The MXene/NCDs was obtained by centrifugal drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MXene/NCDs coatings on Ti6Al4V alloy
ce:section.ce:para: As showed in Fig. 1 , MXene/NCDs coating were grafted onto the surface of the titanium alloy plates by chemical self-assembly. The prepared Ti6Al4V blank alloy samples were first reacted at 20 % H2SO4 solution for 30 min at ambient temperature. After the oxide films were removed, the surfaces were treated. In short, the plates washed with deionized water and dried by N2, and each abstersion should repeat 2–3 times. Then, the dried samples were placed in 5 mol·L−1 KOH solution at 40 °C for 12 h to increase the amount of hydroxyl groups grafted on the surfaces. Afterwards, the hydroxylated titanium alloy plates were placed into 3 % APTES (pH = 8.5) aqueous solution (adjusted by the addition HCl) for 1 h at room temperature, and then the samples were thoroughly stirred in 2 g·L−1 DA Tris solution (pH = 8.5) for 12 h to introduce the amino group. Finally, the samples were respectively laid in 2 g·L−1 MXene/NCDs and 2 g·L−1 MXene aqueous solution. After stirring reacted at 60 °C for 24 h, the obtained samples were respectively abbreviated as Ti-MXene/NCDs and Ti-MXene. The schematic diagram of self-assembly process was shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical corrosion and tribological performances tests
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown the tribological performance probability diagram in Fig. 7(a), the tribological behaviors of these titanium alloy samples during SBF lubrication were stored by a tribometer (UMT-3, Bruker) with a reciprocating ball-on-plate module. The Si3N4 balls with diameter (Φ) of 6 mm, R
a of 0.02 μm and the Vickers hardness of 1500 HV was selected as the upper friction pair. The sliding experiment used a reciprocating module. The samples were measured at a loading of 2 N, the frequency was 1 Hz, and an amplitude of 4 mm. The Ti6Al4V plates were fixed in the sample container with SBF solution as filling liquid. Each test was performed more than three times. The chemical compositions of SBF solution were exhibited in Table 2 .

During friction process, the wear rate (k) of those sample plates were calculated by Eq. (1) [7]: (mm3/(N·m)). Besides, the other weightless method was also applied to calculated the wear rate, as seen in Supporting Information (section 1).
(1)
k =
V
X ∙
F N
Among that, the combined worn volumes of Si3N4 and substrate (V), the sliding distance (X), the vertical load (FN) was input for calculation.

The chemical structures of MXene/NCDs coatings and MXene coatings were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Nicolet In10, USA), 0.1 mg MXene sample was mixed with 10 mg KBr to make a tablet, and then the transmission infrared test was carried out. And the corresponding surface morphologies of these products were utilized by scan electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova 450, USA) at a voltage of 10 kV and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi S-4800 Japan). The morphologies and size of MXene nanosheets were observe by AFM (Multimode Nanoscope IIIa, DI, USA). The static contact Angle (CA) of each sample was measured by a contact Angle surface contactor (OCA20, Dataphysics, German) at least 6 times (As showed in Fig. S1). The elastic modulus of MXene/NCDs coating was measured by nanoindentation tester (NHT³, Anton Paar, Switzerland), and the relevant surface contact pressure was calculated. The crystal pattern of the prepared samples was determined by X-ray Diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Germany) with a scanning speed of 5°/min and a range of 5–90°. Raman spectra of MXene and MXene/NCDs sample powders were obtained by laser Raman excitation at 532 nm at dark room temperature (BWS415-532H, B&W Tek, USA). Moreover, the chemical composition of the worn surfaces was detected by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS).

Fig. 2 characterized the chemical structure of MXene and MXene/NCDs. The absorption peaks of the MXene and MXene/NCDs products were exhibited in Fig. 2(a). In the FTIR spectrum of MXene, the characteristic peaks of -OH, -C=O, and -O were respectively detected at 3409 cm−1, 1636 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1. The characteristic peak of -F etched from HF acid was also evident at 606 cm−1. This implied the successful preparation of MXene with Ti3AlC2. Fig. 2(b) showed the FTIR spectrum of the NCDs, and the characteristic peaks were marked significantly. The peaks of 3550 cm−1 correspond to ν-N-H and ν-O-H bonds and ν-C-H bending vibrations, respectively, further revealing the presence of amine groups. Peak at 1635 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 were respectively represented the stretching vibration of ν-C=O and the bending vibration of ν-C-O bonds in the spectrum, due to the presence of carboxylic acid [62]. The presence of active groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of NCDs can improve the reactivity of NCDs. Compared with the MXene, FTIR spectrum of MXene/NCDs in Fig. 2(a) presented that the characteristic peak at 3181 cm−1 might derive form the ν-N-H of NCDs. These characteristic data suggesting that MXene/NCDs had been successfully synthesized [63].

Through the analysis of XRD spectrum, the successful synthesis of MXene was proved in Fig. 2(c) (the Ti3C2 was marked “∗” and the TiC was marked “♠”). It can be found from Fig. 2(c) that in contrast to the Ti3AlC2, Al characteristic peak disappeared in MXene spectrum [60,64]. Comparing the spectra of the two groups in Fig. 2(c), the disappearance of the (104) peak from Ti3AlC2 confirmed successful etching by HF acid, which has peeled off the Al layer to obtain single Ti3C2 layers. According to previous literature [65], the Ti3AlC2 (002) peak will exhibit broadening, intensity loss, and a shift towards lower angles after removing the Al layer. The value of 002 crystal in MXene shifts left from 10 θ to 8 θ [66], indicating the layer spacing of Ti3C2 increases and the layer thickness decreases, and proved that MXene has been synthesized. This is consistent with the results of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy [67].

The images in Fig. 3(a1) - (a3) and 3 (b1) - (b3) manifested the TEM micrographs and EDX mapping analysis of MXene and MXene/NCDs coatings. The prepared MXene powders were single and lamellar structure with the length of plane approximately 50 nm (Fig. 3(a1)). Furthermore, the distributions of the Ti and Al were obversed from the corresponding mapping of element in Fig. 3(a2) and 3(a3). Among them, Al element almost not be found, implying the successful etching of MXene. The TEM image of MXene/NCDs exhibited that the black dots was uniformly distributed around the surface of MXene (Fig. 3(b1)). The distribution of Ti and N elements (Fig. 3(b2) and 3(b3)) was matched with Fig. 3(b1), indicating the MXene/NCDs was prepared successfully. Fig. 3(c1) presented the monolayer and multilayer structure of MXene thin slices by atomic force microscopy. It can be seen from Fig. 3(c2) that the dimensions of single-layer MXene are: X = 353.70 nm, y = 257.23 nm, Z = 10.71 nm. In general, the thickness of a single layer of Ti3C2Tx MXene film is typically around 1 nm. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis of morphology (Fig. 3(c2)) exhibited a sheet thickness of 10 nm, confirming that the synthesized MXene has a few-layer structure (approximately 10 layers).

The successful grafting of MXene/NCDs onto titanium alloy was verified by SEM morphology analysis, EDX data analysis, EDX mapping images, the thickness of MXene/NCDs coating was analyzed by 3D white light interference in Fig. 4 , and the XPS survey spectra of MXene/NCDs were shown in Fig. 5 . In the SEM image (Fig. 4(a)), many aggregated dots covered on the MXene. This morphology was consistent with the tested result in Fig. 3(b1) above. In addition, it can be observed that MXene/NCDs were uniformly distributed on the titanium alloy plate, and the coating thickness was approximately 8.45 μm (Fig. 4(d)). The roughness (R
a ) of Ti-MXene/NCDs sample obtained by 3D white light interferometer is 3.82 μm, the Ti-MXene is 5.589 μm. The corresponding EDX (Fig. 4(b)) and mapping (Fig. 4(c)) exhibited the content of each element and their distribution on the coating. The proportion of N in the EDS data was approximately 0.7, and the abundant distribution maps of Ti, C, and N elements (Fig. 4(c)) indicated the grafting of MXene/NCDs coatings onto the surface of Ti6Al4V.

In addition, Fig. 5 showed the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the MXene/NCDs coating, displayed Ti2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks. As presented in Fig. 5(a), Ti-C 2p3 (455.8eV) and 2p1 (461.67eV) are the characteristic peaks of Ti3C2, and 458.07 eV indicated Ti-O bonds resulting from surface oxidation [70,71]. In addition, the peak at 456.96eV may be Ti-N bond (Ti (III)) [72]. In Fig. 5(b), peaks at 531.77 and 532.89 eV represented C-O and C=O bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5(c)) exhibits peaks at 400.24 and 401.92 eV, confirming the presence of C-N and N-H bonds [73]. Furthermore, Fig. 5(d) illustrated C1s peaks at 284.61 eV (C-C bonds), 286.04 eV (C-O and C-N bonds), and C=O bonds, corroborating the SEM and EDS analyses and confirming successful grafting of MXene/NCDs onto the Ti substrate [74].

Electrochemical corrosion tests were measured on the surface of Ti, Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs samples. Then, the analysis of results was shown in Fig. 6
. It can be found from the Nyquist plots diagrams impedance of three samples (Fig. 6(a)) that Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs exhibited larger radius of curvature than uncoated Ti6Al4V samples. Among them, the largest radius of curvature was MXene/NCDs. It was suggested that the coating surface had the slower electrochemical reaction rate, resulting in electrochemical corrosion resistance and protection of the Ti6Al4V substrate, while the MXene/NCDs coating was the best. That possibly due to carbon quantum dots dispersedly filled the MXene surface, reducing the ions entered into the simulated body to participate in the reaction. The tafel curves of the Ti6Al4V, Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs samples presented similar variation trends (Fig. 6(b)). Referring to the previous study, the smaller the corrosion current, the better the corrosion resistance. The corrosion potential and corrosion current density of the three samples were detailly equipped with Table 3 . And the sequence of E
corr was as follows: Ti-MXene/NCDs (−314 mV) > Ti-MXene (−416 mV) > Ti6Al4V (−451 mV). These results suggesting that MXene/NCDs coatings ameliorated the corrosion resistance of titanium alloy substrates. The corrosion resistance of MXene and MXene/NCDs coatings can also be demonstrated by the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η%). The formula was calculated according to:

As showed in Table 3, the inhibition efficiency of coated plates increased to 82.87 % (Ti-MXene) and 94.27 % (Ti-MXene/NCDs). revealing that the corrosion resistance of MXene/NCDs coating was excellent. Therefore, it can be concluded that the MXene/NCDs coating was the optimum corrosion barrier to effectively prevent the corrosion process of Ti6Al4V alloy in SBF solution.

The tribological behaviors of all samples were measured in SBF lubrications. The change of friction coefficient (COF) with time was presented in Fig. 7(b). Fig. 7(c) exhibited the average coefficient of friction (ACOF), and the wear rate data (k) of three samples though the calculation of the formula were displayed in Fig. 7(d). According to Fig. 7(b), the curve of the Ti-MXene/NCDs and uncoated Ti6Al4V samples appeared a trend of rising run-off stage in the first 600 s. The trend of Ti-MXene/NCDs curve showed the lowest data from 600 s–1800 s, possibly due to MXene played a lubricating role and the curve began to decline. From the perspective of the entire friction curve, the uncoated Ti6Al4V sample characterized a fluctuating trend, this was ascribed the surface of titanium alloy was relatively rough. When the actual contact area increasing, large fragments fell off the surface in the friction, and the coefficient of friction increased. After that, large fragments wore and became small fragments, transformed into sliding friction, the friction coefficient decreased, and finally became stable wear. Compared to the uncoated Ti6Al4V samples, Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs basically appeared a stable trend in the friction, mainly due to MXene played a lubricating role in whole friction process. Comparing the friction coefficients of the three curves, the value of Ti-MXene/NCDs friction coefficient was the lowest. It can be summarized that the wear resistance of the Ti-MXene/NCDs was the first-rank.

The average friction coefficient (ACOF) under the SBF conditions was intuitively compared in Fig. 7(c). All the data of friction coefficient were obtained after many experiments, excluding chance. The average friction coefficients of Ti6Al4V, Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs three samples were in accord with the analysis results in Fig. 7(b). The ACOF of Ti-MXene/NCDs coating was significantly lower than that of uncoated and only contained MXene samples, mean that Ti-MXene/NCDs coating has excellent friction resistance.

The wear rates of three samples were calculated by white-light interferometer analysis in Fig. 7(d). It can be observed from the figure that the wear rates of Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs were distinctly reduced in SBF lubrication friction. The same regularity of wear rates results was observed in Table S1 using the weightless method. Moreover, the wearability of the latter was better, indicating that MXene/NCDs coating has the best protective effect on titanium alloy. Comparing to the other two-dimensional materials in the pervious published reports [23,29], MXene/NCDs exhibited excellent tribological properties. The comparative tribological properties were listed in Table S2.

The microscopic observation and surface composition analysis results of the worn surfaces of the three samples were shown in Fig. 8 . The 3D images (Fig. 8(a1) - 8(c1)) intuitively exhibited a decreasing trend of wear depth: Ti (8 μm), Ti-MXene (6 μm) and Ti-MXene/NCDs (approximately 1 μm). Ti-MXene/NCDs presented the smallest wear scar depth and the lightest wear degree. Moreover, the corresponding SEM images of on these samples wear scars can be observed in Fig. 8(a2) - 8(c2) and Fig. 8(a3) - 8(c3). The sequence of surface wear scar diameter followed: Ti > Ti-MXene > Ti-MXene/NCDs. As displayed in Fig. 8(c3), the width of Ti-MXene/NCDs was nearly 3 times smaller than that of titanium alloy, illustrating that Ti-MXene/NCDs was equiped with significant wear resistance. Seen the enlarged view Fig. 8(a3), Fig. 8(b3), and Fig. 8(c3), the ploughs, scratches and debris appeared in the enlarged image of the wear marks on the Ti-MXene sample are smaller than those on the uncoating Ti6Al4V sample, while the Ti-MXene/NCDs only stood out some ploughs. It was suggested that the abrasive wear and adhesive wear both occurred in the friction process between uncoating and MXene titanium alloys. Although MXene played a certain lubrication role to reduce the value, the carbon quantum dots added to the MXene coating can more effectively impact the surface wear of Ti6Al4V samples. It mainly attributed to the shed carbon quantum dots entered the MXene crevices, resulting in the reduce of abrasive wear and adhesive wear. Additionally, the friction mechanism was summarized from the EDX analysis diagram of three samples surface (Fig. 8(a4) - 8(c4)). The presence of Si and N elements on uncoated Ti6Al4V samples revealed that Si and N elements from the upper friction pair (Si3N4) were transferred to the surface of the Ti6Al4V sample, and proving that the adhesive wear appeared on the Ti6Al4V sample. As EDX analysis table exhibited on Fig. 8(b4), more carbon elements were seen, suggested that there was still a large amount of MXene on the surface of Ti-MXene, and little slight wear on the coating. The EDX analysis of Ti-MXene/NCDs (Fig. 8(c4)) contained a large amount of nitrogen and carbon elements, concluded that the few worn trace on the surface of Ti-MXene/NCDs was consistent with SEM and 3D topography analysis. The analysis of worn surface also corresponded to the wear rates calculated above (Fig. 7(d)), suggesting that Ti-MXene/NCDs primely reduced wear and improved friction performance.

Fig. 9 presented the Raman spectra of worn surfaces from Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs. Among them, spectra 203 cm−1 and 205 cm−1 corresponded to the Ti-O bond of the coatings, 416 cm−1 and 420 cm−1 represented the Ti-OH bond of coatings. peaks at 600 cm−1, 820 cm−1and 577 cm−1, 780 cm−1 were represented Ti-C bond of MXene and MXene/NCDs coatings [68,69]. The results indicated that those MXene and MXene/NCDs were detected on those wear scars, implying the occurrence and integrity of MXene and MXene/NCDs after the tribological test.

As shown in Figs. 10, 3D white light interference, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electron energy spectrum (EDX) were used to characterize and detect the components of the ball friction surface under the condition of SBF. It can be observed from Fig. 10(a1) - 10(c1) that compared with the Si3N4 ball rubbing with blank Ti plates, the Ti-MXene/NCDs showed smaller wear scar, which is consistent with the microstructure of wear scar from the titanium alloy plates. While the wear depths of three group Si3N4 balls rubbing with different titanium alloy samples have no significant difference, which is related to the high mechanical properties of Si3N4 ball. In addition, the SEM images (Fig. 10(a2) - 10(c2)) on the worn surfaces of different groups Si3N4 balls exhibited the obvious distinction. Among that, the Si3N4 ball rubbing with blank Ti plates possessed larger wear scar than that against the titanium alloy plate with MXene/NCDs coatings. The magnification SEM images presented (Fig. 10(a3) - 10(c3)) that compared to the blank Ti group, the Si3N4 ball against Ti-MXene/NCDs almost had no noticeable scratch, plough and debris, implying the protect effect of the MXene/NCDs coating from severe wear. Besides, the elemental analysis on the worn surface of Si3N4 balls were conducted using EDX and shown in Fig. 10(a4) - 10(c4). It was significant that the Al, V elements were detected on those wear scars, indicating the transfer from the titanium alloys plates to Si3N4 balls, implying the adhesive wear mechanisms.

In this study, MXene/NCDs coating was successfully prepared on the surface of titanium alloy, and the experimental results illustrated that the corrosion resistance and tribological properties of the MXene coatings doped with NCDs on the titanium alloy matrix were significantly improved. In addition, the lowest electrochemical corrosion current density and higher inhibition efficiency of Ti-MXene/NCDs, suggesting that the MXene/NCDs coating presented excellent anticorrosion properties. The anticorrosion mechanisms and tribological mechanisms of the coating were detailedly analyzed as follows.

As recently mentioned [21], the reaction on the surface of titanium oxide primarily caused by the corrosion of chloride ions and sulfate ions present in human body fluids. Therefore, Ti6Al4V coated with MXene/NCDs may exhibit the following corrosion and degradation reactions in SBF solution in SBF solution might appear the following reactions in Fig. 11 [76]. Our preceding research [19,21] proved that APTES and DA transition layers can block active ions and prevent corrosion of titanium alloys. Secondly, MXene nanomaterials contained high corrosion resistance due to their bionic, layered physical property structure and surface. The rich hydroxyl group on its surface allows limited transport of water and ions in SBF, the synergistic effect between high aspect ratio and non-permeability, and the extension of the diffusion of corrosive ions (Cl−, SO4 2−) by its own nanosheets all effectively enhanced the corrosion resistance of MXene. Carbon quantum dots as the effective corrosion inhibitor, were adsorbed on the exposed MXene surface, greatly enhancing the dispersion and interfacial compatibility of MXene in the coating. As showed in Fig. 11, the doping of the N element introduced new functional groups (-NH2, -COOH) and reacted with ions in SBF, effectively reducing the ions involved in corrosion.

The surface properties and microstructure of coatings both seriously affect the tribological properties. The results have suggested that the friction coefficient and wear rate of the Ti6Al4V samples were memorably reduced by the MXene/NCDs coating. The surface of MXene/NCDs contained with a large number of groups (-COOH, -NH2) can form a lubricating film with ions (Cl−, SO4 2−) in SBF solution, reducing the friction coefficient. As previous research described, friction was related to shear stress (Formulas 1, 2, 3) [21]. MXene coating can reduce shear stress(τ) by reducing the actual contact area in SBF lubricant friction [76]. Under lubrication conditions, the coefficient of friction (COF) was positively correlated with the normal force (Ff,adh). It may be another factor in the reduction of the coefficient of friction. Abrasive wear, adhesive wear and corrosion wear are common mechanisms. The wear of MXene surface results (Fig. 8(b3) and Fig. 10(b3)) appeared shallow ploughshares and small chips, indicating that MXene tended to form a wear-resistant friction layer, and resulting in the reduction of abrasive force (Ff,pl) and abrasive wear rate. This may due to the low shear strength and interlaminar lubrication of MXene [21,24,47]. It can be seen from Table 4 that compared with the blank Ti plate, the contact pressure and max shear stress of MXene was respectively reduced by 14.23 % and 14.22 % after the coated MXene, the contact pressure of MXene/NCDs coating was respectively reduced by 4.3 % and 9.83 %. This may be attributed to the NCDs modified the defects of MXene and improved the mechanical strength of MXene.

This study employed chemical self-assembly techniques to meticulously prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) modified MXene (MXene/NCDs) composite coatings on Ti6Al4V alloy surfaces. Ti-MXene and Ti-MXene/NCDs presented enhanced biotribological and anti-corrosion properties in simulated body fluid (SBF) lubricants than the bare Ti6Al4V alloy, especially the MXene/NCDs coating showed superior performance. The promoted biotribological performances of MXene/NCDs coating might be ascribed to the reduced contact pressure and shear forces at the friction interface of the coated titanium alloys, with a “dual-action” friction reduction and wear mechanism involving the sliding of MXene coatings and rolling of NCDs. Furthermore, the improved corrosion resistance of MXene/NCDs coatings was attributed to their effective shielding mechanism against active ions (Cl−, SO4 2−) in the solution. These consequences highlight the promising applications of MXene/NCDs coatings in enhancing wear resistance and corrosion protection in biomedical applications.
